---
title: "On the Logistic Curve"
subtitle: "A note on saturation in epidemic models"
author: "John Drake"
institute: "Odum School of Ecology"
date: today
format:
  drake-navy-revealjs:
    slide-number: c/t
    transition: none
    incremental: false
---

# Opening {.divider}

## A simple question

Why does the logistic equation appear so often in epidemiology, ecology,
and the social sciences alike?

The answer turns less on biology than on a structural property of
constrained growth.

::: {.fragment}
We can sketch this in three slides.
:::

## Setup

Consider a population $N(t)$ growing at rate $r$ in a system with
carrying capacity $K$:

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} = rN \left( 1 - \frac{N}{K} \right) $$

The right-hand side carries two ideas: exponential growth ($rN$)
and a brake ($1 - N/K$) that engages as the population approaches
its limit.

## Behavior over time {.figure-slide}

![](figures/logistic-curve.png){fig-align="center"}

<p class="caption">Solution to the logistic ODE for varying initial conditions. *r* = 0.3, *K* = 1000.</p>

## A quote {.divider}

> All models are wrong, but some are useful.
>
> — George Box

## Code example

```r
library(deSolve)

logistic <- function(t, N, parms) {
  with(as.list(parms), list(r * N * (1 - N / K)))
}

out <- ode(y = c(N = 10), times = seq(0, 50, 0.1),
           func = logistic, parms = c(r = 0.3, K = 1000))
```

## Background image with frosted caption

::: {background-image="figures/field.jpg" background-size="cover"}

::: {.frosted}
A long-term study site in the southern Appalachians, where logistic
dynamics emerge from competition for limited light.
:::

:::

## Closing {.divider}

*Thank you.*

jdrake@uga.edu
